student_message: A butterfly? Is the line a minus sign? This is too hard.
final_message: Math can definitely be hard sometimes, Riya, so let's take a deep breath together. You did a wonderful job earlier calling it a fold line and picturing a butterfly! Remembering words is tricky, and making mistakes is just a normal part of learning. I completely believe in you! That line isn't a minus sign, but that was a very brave guess. The magic math word for that fold line is a line of symmetry. A line of symmetry is just a special fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Let's imagine you are designing a magical butterfly. If you paint $$4$$ glowing purple spots on the left wing, and it has a perfect line of symmetry down the middle, how many spots will be on the right wing? You can solve this mirror mystery! 🦋💛

orchestrator_intent.goal: Comfort Riya, regain her attention, and gently explain the term line of symmetry.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: Riya is sad, looking away, and feeling overwhelmed. She needs encouragement for her sadness, engagement to bring her focus back, and dialogue to clarify the vocabulary.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
session_summary: The session covered symmetry and symmetrical letters. Riya visually understands symmetry, successfully recognizing matching parts and "fold lines." However, she struggles to identify symmetrical letters (guessing R and P) and recall formal vocabulary. Her current learning state is highly frustrated and overwhelmed. Exhibiting negative self-talk, she repeatedly states the material is "too hard." She lacks confidence and requires constant encouragement to connect her accurate visual understanding to the correct mathematical terms.
